content.body: Genetics is the study of heredity — how characteristics are passed from parents to offspring. The key molecules carrying genetic information are DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid), organised into chromosomes and genes.

Key definitions:
DNA: double helix molecule made of nucleotides (sugar + phosphate + base). Carries genetic code.
Gene: a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein or trait.
Chromosome: tightly coiled DNA + protein structure. Humans have 46 chromosomes (23 pairs).
Allele: different versions of the same gene (e.g. tall vs short allele for height).
Locus: the specific position of a gene on a chromosome.

content.title: DNA Structure
content.body:
DNA is a double helix — two strands wound around each other.
Base pairing rules (ALWAYS pair the same way):
Adenine (A) pairs with Thymine (T)
Guanine (G) pairs with Cytosine (C)

If one strand reads: A-T-G-C-A
The complementary strand reads: T-A-C-G-T

Genotype vs Phenotype:
Genotype: the genetic make-up (alleles present). e.g. Tt, TT, tt
Phenotype: the physical appearance/characteristic. e.g. tall or short

Dominant allele (T): expressed even if only one copy present
Recessive allele (t): only expressed if TWO copies present

Homozygous dominant: TT (tall)
Heterozygous: Tt (tall — dominant expressed)
Homozygous recessive: tt (short)

content.title: Monohybrid Cross — Punnett Square
content.body:
Cross two heterozygous tall plants (Tt × Tt):

     T    t
T  [ TT ] [ Tt ]
t  [ Tt ] [ tt ]

Results: TT : Tt : tt = 1 : 2 : 1
Phenotype ratio: 3 tall : 1 short
75% tall, 25% short

questions.question: How many chromosomes do human body cells contain?
questions.explanation: Humans have 46 chromosomes in 23 pairs in each body (somatic) cell.

questions.question: What does a gene code for?
questions.explanation: A gene is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein (or trait).

questions.question: Which bases pair together in DNA?
questions.explanation: Base pairing rules: Adenine pairs with Thymine (A-T), Guanine pairs with Cytosine (G-C).

questions.question: A plant with genotype Tt is:
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means having two different alleles (one dominant, one recessive) for a gene.

questions.question: If T (tall) is dominant and t (short) is recessive, what is the phenotype of Tt?
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed even when only one copy is present. Tt is tall.

questions.question: In a Tt × Tt cross, what proportion of offspring are tt?
questions.explanation: From Punnett square: TT, Tt, Tt, tt. One out of four (1/4 = 25%) is tt.

questions.question: What is the phenotype ratio from a Tt × Tt cross?
questions.explanation: 3 tall (TT + Tt + Tt) : 1 short (tt) = 3:1 phenotype ratio.

questions.question: Phenotype refers to:
options: The physical characteristics an organism shows
questions.answer: The physical characteristics an organism shows
questions.explanation: Phenotype is the observable physical characteristics (appearance) of an organism.

questions.question: Different versions of the same gene are called:
questions.explanation: Alleles are different forms/versions of the same gene (e.g. tall allele T and short allele t).

questions.question: A cross between TT and tt gives offspring genotype:
questions.explanation: TT × tt: every offspring gets T from one parent and t from the other. All offspring are Tt.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Sex in humans is determined by sex chromosomes. Females have two X chromosomes (XX). Males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY). The 23rd pair of human chromosomes are the sex chromosomes.

Sex Determination Cross:
Mother (XX) × Father (XY)

     X    X
X  [ XX ] [ XX ]
Y  [ XY ] [ XY ]

Result: 50% XX (female) : 50% XY (male)
The FATHER determines the sex of the child!

Sex-linked characteristics are controlled by genes carried on the sex chromosomes (usually the X chromosome). Because males have only ONE X chromosome, they only need ONE copy of a recessive allele to show the trait. Females need TWO copies (one on each X).

content.title: Colour Blindness (X-linked)
content.body:
Colour blindness gene is on the X chromosome.
Let Xᴺ = normal vision (dominant)
Let Xⁿ = colour blind (recessive)

Female carrier (XᴺXⁿ) × Normal male (XᴺY):

       Xᴺ    Xⁿ
Xᴺ  [XᴺXᴺ] [XᴺXⁿ]
Y   [XᴺY]  [XⁿY]

XᴺXᴺ = normal female
XᴺXⁿ = carrier female (normal vision)
XᴺY = normal male
XⁿY = colour blind male (50% of sons!)

Other X-linked conditions: haemophilia (blood clotting disorder), Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
Why are males more often affected? They cannot be carriers — they either have the condition (Xⁿ Y) or not (Xᴺ Y). Females can be carriers (XᴺXⁿ) and appear normal.

questions.question: What are the sex chromosomes of a human female?
questions.explanation: Human females have two X chromosomes (XX). Males have XY.

questions.question: Who determines the sex of a child?
questions.explanation: The mother can only give X. The father gives either X (→girl) or Y (→boy), so the father's sperm determines sex.

questions.question: What percentage of children from any pregnancy are expected to be male?
questions.explanation: XX × XY cross always gives 50% XX (female) and 50% XY (male).

questions.question: Why are males more often colour blind than females?
options: Males have only one X chromosome, so one recessive allele causes the condition
Females can never be colour blind
The gene is on the Y chromosome
questions.answer: Males have only one X chromosome, so one recessive allele causes the condition
questions.explanation: Males (XY) have only one X. One copy of the recessive colour blind allele (Xⁿ) is enough to cause colour blindness. Females need two copies.

questions.question: A woman with genotype XᴺXⁿ is called:
questions.explanation: A carrier has the recessive allele but doesn't show the condition (because the dominant allele masks it). She can pass it to her children.

questions.question: X-linked genes are located on:
questions.explanation: X-linked (sex-linked) characteristics are controlled by genes on the X chromosome.

questions.question: A colour blind father (XⁿY) and normal homozygous mother (XᴺXᴺ). What are the sons' genotypes?
questions.explanation: Sons get Y from father and Xᴺ from mother. All sons: XᴺY = normal vision.

questions.question: From the same cross (XⁿY father × XᴺXᴺ mother), what are the daughters?
questions.explanation: Daughters get Xⁿ from father and Xᴺ from mother → all daughters are XᴺXⁿ (carriers).

questions.question: Haemophilia is caused by:
questions.explanation: Haemophilia is caused by a recessive allele on the X chromosome — it is sex-linked.

questions.question: Can a colour blind woman exist?
options: Yes — if she has genotype XⁿXⁿ
Only if she inherits Y from her father
Only if she has one X chromosome
questions.answer: Yes — if she has genotype XⁿXⁿ
questions.explanation: A female can be colour blind if she inherits a recessive colour blind allele from BOTH parents (XⁿXⁿ), though this is rare.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body:
Monohybrid cross: one gene, two alleles
Dominant allele (R) masks recessive (r)
Possible genotypes: RR (homozygous dominant), Rr (heterozygous), rr (homozygous recessive)
Phenotype ratios:
Rr × Rr → 3 dominant : 1 recessive (3:1)
Rr × rr → 1 dominant : 1 recessive (1:1) — test cross

content.title: Codominance and Multiple Alleles
content.body:
Codominance: both alleles expressed equally
Example: Blood groups (ABO) — IA, IB, i
IAIB = blood group AB (both expressed)
IAi = blood group A, IBi = group B, ii = group O

Sickle cell anaemia: HbA HbS → carrier (some sickle cells)
HbS HbS → sickle cell disease

Sex determination:
Humans: XX = female, XY = male
X-linked inheritance: gene on X chromosome
X-linked recessive: females need 2 copies to show, males only need 1
Examples: colour blindness, haemophilia
Carrier female (X^N X^n) × normal male (X^N Y)
→ 1/4 affected male, 1/4 carrier female, 1/4 normal male, 1/4 normal female

questions.question: A heterozygous individual has...
questions.explanation: Heterozygous = two different alleles for a gene (e.g. Rr). Homozygous = two identical alleles (RR or rr).

questions.question: In a Rr × Rr cross, the expected phenotype ratio is...
questions.explanation: Punnett square: RR, Rr, Rr, rr → 3 dominant phenotype : 1 recessive phenotype.

questions.question: A test cross determines genotype by crossing with...
questions.explanation: Cross unknown with rr. If any recessive offspring appear → unknown must carry recessive allele (is Rr).

questions.question: In codominance, the heterozygote shows...
questions.explanation: In codominance (e.g. ABO blood groups), heterozygotes express BOTH alleles equally — neither is dominant over the other.

questions.question: Blood type AB indicates genotype...
questions.explanation: Blood group AB has both A and B antigens → genotype IAIB (both alleles expressed by codominance).

questions.question: Sex-linked (X-linked) recessive conditions are more common in males because...
options: Males only have one X — one recessive allele is enough to show the trait
questions.answer: Males only have one X — one recessive allele is enough to show the trait
questions.explanation: Males are XY — only one X chromosome. If that X carries the recessive allele, it's expressed (no second X to 'mask' it).

questions.question: Haemophilia is X-linked recessive. A carrier mother (X^H X^h) × normal father (X^H Y). Probability of affected son =
questions.explanation: Sons are XY. 50% of sons get X^h (affected). Sons = 50% of children. So 25% of all children are affected sons.

questions.question: Sickle cell carrier (HbA HbS) has...
options: Some sickle cells but mild symptoms; malaria-resistant
questions.answer: Some sickle cells but mild symptoms; malaria-resistant
questions.explanation: Carrier has one normal and one sickle gene. Has some sickle cells under stress but lives normally. HbS confers malaria resistance — explains why sickle cell is common in malaria regions.

questions.question: Polygenic inheritance means a trait is controlled by...
questions.explanation: Polygenic traits (e.g. height, skin colour) are controlled by multiple genes, each with a small effect — giving continuous variation.

questions.question: A gene mutation...
options: Alters the DNA sequence of a gene
questions.answer: Alters the DNA sequence of a gene
questions.explanation: A mutation is a permanent change in the DNA base sequence of a gene. Most mutations are neutral; some affect protein function.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Genetics & Inheritance. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Genetics & Inheritance concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Genetics & Inheritance builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Genetics & Inheritance. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Genetics & Inheritance. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Genetics & Inheritance include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Genetics & Inheritance. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Genetics & Inheritance. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Genetics & Inheritance concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Genetics & Inheritance builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Genetics & Inheritance. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Genetics & Inheritance. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Genetics & Inheritance include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Genetics & Inheritance. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
